mnemonics.title: TCP/IP Layers Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: AUNT: Application, UDP/TCP, Network, Too many acronyms! Alternative: All People Tend To Need Data Processing - Application, Presentation, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical. For TCP/IP: A-T-I-N (Application-Transport-Internet-Network)

mnemonics.title: DHCP DORA Process
mnemonics.content: DORA: Discover (client broadcasts), Offer (server offers IP), Request (client requests), Acknowledge (server acknowledges). Remember: Dogs Often Run Around!

mnemonics.title: Well-Known Ports
mnemonics.content: FTP=21, SSH=22, Telnet=23, SMTP=25, DNS=53, DHCP=67/68, HTTP=80, POP3=110, HTTPS=443. Mnemonic: FTP (21) SSH (22) TELnet (23) SMTP (25) - Numbers increase roughly sequentially!

mnemonics.title: TCP Three-Way Handshake
mnemonics.content: SYN -> SYN-ACK -> ACK. Remember: 'SYN' sounds like 'SIN' - need to apologize (send ACK) to start a conversation!

acronyms.usage: Connection-oriented, reliable protocol for data transfer between applications.
Provides logical addressing and routing of packets across networks.
Connectionless, unreliable protocol for fast data transfer.
Maps IP addresses to MAC addresses on local networks.
Maps MAC addresses to IP addresses (mostly obsolete).
Translates domain names to IP addresses.
Automatically assigns IP addresses to network devices.
Protocol for file transfer between client and server.
IP addressing scheme replacing classful addressing.

visualTips.visualization: Draw two stacks side-by-side. TCP/IP has 4 boxes: Application (combines OSI 5,6,7), Transport (OSI 4), Internet (OSI 3), Network Access (combines OSI 1,2). Draw arrows showing how they correspond.
Draw a timeline: Client sends SYN (→), Server responds SYN-ACK (←), Client sends ACK (→). Label each step. Show how sequence numbers are exchanged.
Draw data packet as an onion. Start with Data at center, wrap with Transport header (segment), wrap with Internet header (packet), wrap with Network header (frame). Each layer adds its own header!

Draw four steps in sequence: 1. Discover (client broadcasts 'Any DHCP server?'), 2. Offer (server says 'I can give you x.x.x.x'), 3. Request (client says 'I'll take x.x.x.x'), 4. Acknowledge (server says 'OK, it's yours').
Draw number line 0-223. Class A: 1-126 (large networks, /8), Class B: 128-191 (medium, /16), Class C: 192-223 (small, /24). Class D (multicast) 224-239, Class E 240-255. Note: 127 is loopback!

analogies.analogy: TCP is like making a phone call - you dial, wait for connection, talk, then hang up. UDP is like sending a postcard - you write address and mail it, no confirmation it arrived.
IP address is like a postal address (street name + house number) - identifies location. MAC address is like your face - unique to you, identifies YOU (hardware).
Default gateway is like the main entrance/exit of a building. All traffic going outside the local area must pass through it.
DNS is like a phonebook - you look up a name (google.com) to find the phone number (IP address) to call.
A router is like a traffic policeman at an intersection, directing packets to their correct destination networks.
